metadata.title: Guideline on the evaluation of medicinal products for the treatment of chronic constipation (including opioid induced constipation) and for bowel cleansing
metadata.notes: Final version adopted by CHMP 25 June 2015; supersedes draft EMA/CHMP/336243/2013 (Feb 2014) already extracted to ema-draft-guideline-chronic-constipation.json. Covers three indication types: chronic idiopathic constipation (CIC), opioid-induced constipation (OIC), and bowel cleansing. Rules extracted from main normative text; background/context paragraphs excluded.
metadata.replaces: ema-draft-guideline-chronic-constipation.json (draft, 2014-02-20, 28 rules — SUPERSEDED by this final version)

rules.rule_text: Treatment claims for chronic constipation must clearly specify 'chronic functional constipation' or 'chronic idiopathic constipation' (CIC) to state that secondary constipation has not been investigated for the compound.
rules.verbatim_quote: A treatment claim for chronic constipation should therefore in the future clearly be specified as 'chronic functional constipation' or 'chronic idiopathic constipation' (CIC), in order to state that secondary constipation has not been investigated for the compound.
rules.exceptions: Not required if the applicant can demonstrate studies in secondary constipation to support a broader claim.

rules.rule_text: A broad claim of 'chronic constipation' (without restriction to idiopathic or functional) is only possible if relevant studies have also been conducted in secondary constipation.
rules.verbatim_quote: A broad claim of 'chronic constipation' is only possible, if relevant studies have also been conducted in secondary constipation.

rules.rule_text: Secondary constipation (due to underlying disease or medication) must usually be documented in separate studies to obtain a general constipation indication beyond idiopathic constipation only. Alternatively, broad studies including all patient types are acceptable if powered to show statistically significant effects in the subgroups.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2 — Secondary constipation including Opioid induced constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: Secondary constipation (due to underlying disease (e.g. endocrine or neurological) or medication) should usually be documented in separate studies in order to obtain a general constipation indication beyond idiopathic constipation only (see 4.1.). However, also broad studies including all types of patients could be regarded to be acceptable, if powered to show statistically significant effects in the subgroups.
rules.exceptions: Broad studies acceptable if powered to demonstrate statistically significant effects in secondary constipation subgroups.

rules.rule_text: The only secondary constipation entity for which a separate indication claim is considered adequate is opioid induced constipation (OIC). A separate indication claim for other secondary constipation entities (e.g. Parkinson's Disease related constipation) is considered unwarranted.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2 — Secondary constipation including Opioid induced constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: The only secondary constipation entity for which a separate claim is considered adequate, is for 'opioid induced constipation' (OIC). This indication can also be claimed if only opioid-induced constipation patients are investigated, especially where – as expected – the mechanism of action of compounds is the specific antagonist activity at the opioid receptor.
rules.exceptions: OIC claim is always acceptable. For other secondary constipation entities, data may lead to inclusion of information in SmPC Chapter 5.1 only.

rules.rule_text: A claim for 'opioid induced bowel dysfunction' (OIBD) must be separated from OIC and requires documentation of effects relating to the upper gastrointestinal tract and/or effects other than constipation and constipation-related complaints. OIBD is outside the scope of this guideline.
rules.source_section: Section 4.2 — Secondary constipation including Opioid induced constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: A claim on 'opioid induced bowel dysfunction' (OIBD) has to be separated from OIC, and would need the documentation of effects also relating to the upper gastrointestinal tract and/or effects other than on constipation and constipation related complaints alone. Constipation related complaints are defined similar to those included in the Rome III definitions. OIBD would however, also encompass reflux related complaints (heartburn, acid regurgitation), epigastric pain, nausea and vomiting, and biliary complaints including colic. This is considered to be outside the scope of this guideline.

rules.rule_text: A claim for treatment of patients with 'previously failed therapy on other (usual) laxatives' must be substantiated in clinical trials. Simple history taking (asking patients whether they previously used and failed laxatives) is not sufficient.
rules.verbatim_quote: Simple history taking – asking patients whether they have indeed used other medicinal products in the past, and whether this has been unsuccessful – is not regarded to be sufficient in this regard. A claim of treatment of patients with 'previously failed therapy on other ("usual") laxatives' will have to be substantiated.

rules.rule_text: Patients with inadequate response to 'usual laxatives' enrolled in clinical trials must confirm insufficient response to laxative treatment with at least two drug substances from different classes (bulking agent/fibre, osmotic laxative, or stimulant laxative) by history taking. Additionally, the inadequate response to at least one agent must be documented during the run-in period.
rules.verbatim_quote: A patient with inadequate response to 'usual laxatives' should confirm insufficient response to laxative treatment with at least two drug substances belonging to different classes used in the treatment of constipation (bulking agent/fibre, osmotic laxative, or stimulant laxative) by history taking. Additionally, the inadequate response to at least one of the agents should be documented during the run-in period of the trials.
rules.exceptions: If a general claim (without specifying line of therapy) is aimed at, studies must be powered to show statistically significant effects in both first-line and previously failed therapy subgroups.

rules.rule_text: Treatment claims for bowel cleansing agents should, as a rule, be related to the treatment of patients scheduled for colonoscopy. Any indication relating to other diagnostic or therapeutic procedures, including surgery, requires additional justification.
rules.verbatim_quote: Treatment claims for bowel cleansing agents should therefore – as a rule – be related to the treatment of patient scheduled for colonoscopy. Any deviating indication – relating to other diagnostic or to therapeutic procedures, including surgery, would need additional justification.
rules.exceptions: Additional justification may be provided to support a broader bowel cleansing claim.

rules.rule_text: In early development of CIC drug candidates, patients should be selected based on Rome III criteria for chronic functional constipation, and a subdivision for patients with slow transit constipation (STC) and normal transit constipation (NTC) must be made.
rules.verbatim_quote: Whereas generally patients may be selected based on the Rome III criteria for chronic functional constipation, a subdivision for patients with slow and with normal transit constipation (Slow Transit Constipation and Normal Transit Constipation; STC and NTC) should be made.
rules.exceptions: In Phase II/III, STC/NTC differentiation is impractical and unnecessary unless differential responses become evident in early development.

rules.rule_text: In early development, patients with defecation disorders must be carefully diagnosed, and the compound must be tested in this population separately or these patients must be excluded from early studies. A full diagnostic workup including transit testing, balloon expulsion test, defecation imaging, manometry, and EMG is recommended.
rules.verbatim_quote: During this early development, it is also considered necessary to carefully diagnose patients with defecation disorders and test the compound for its effects in this patient population separately, or exclude them from these early studies. During this phase, a full diagnostic workup with transit testing balloon expulsion test, defecation imaging, manometry, and EMG is recommended (see Chapter 6.2).

rules.rule_text: In Phase II and Phase III CIC trials, patients with suspected defecation disorders must not be included. Red flag symptoms (new onset of symptoms, anaemia, rectal bleeding, positive faecal blood test, weight loss) must be exclusion criteria.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1.1 — Chronic idiopathic constipation (Phase II and phase III trials)
rules.verbatim_quote: A different approach, however, has to be taken on patients with suspected defecation disorders, which should not be included into clinical trials in this indication. For these patients, indeed a lower response to laxatives has been shown to be likely, and the treatment recommendations are indeed different. [...] so-called 'red flags' should be excluded (new onset of symptoms, anaemia, rectal bleeding, positive faecal blood test, weight loss etc.)

rules.rule_text: CIC patients enrolled in Phase II/III trials must fulfil the following modified Rome III criteria: (a) 2 or more of: straining ≥25% of defecations (severity <3–4 on 5-point scale), lumpy/hard stools ≥25% of defecations, fewer than 3 defecations/week; AND (b) all of: loose stools rarely present without laxatives, normal rectal sphincter tonus on digital rectal examination, insufficient criteria for IBS, no other identified cause of constipation. Additionally, increased sphincter tone must be excluded by digital rectal examination.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1.1 — Chronic idiopathic constipation (Phase II and phase III trials)
rules.verbatim_quote: a) A patient must fulfil 2 or more of the following 3 criteria: Straining during at least 25% of defecations but severity should be less than 3 or 4 on a 5-point scale; Lumpy or hard stools in at least 25% of defecations; fewer than 3 defecations per week. b) A patient must fulfil all of the following: Loose stools are rarely present without the use of laxatives; Normal rectal sphincter tonus on digital rectal examination; There are insufficient criteria for IBS; No other reasons for constipation can be identified (e.g. medication).
rules.exceptions: Patients may additionally suffer from sensation of incomplete evacuation, anorectal obstruction/blockage, or manual manoeuvres ≥25% of defecations (criterion c), but these are not required.

rules.rule_text: During the run-in period of CIC trials, the number of stools must not exceed 5 in two weeks.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1.1 — Chronic idiopathic constipation (Phase II and phase III trials)
rules.verbatim_quote: During the run-in period, the number of stools should not exceed 5 in two weeks.

rules.rule_text: All CIC patients enrolled in trials should be evaluated for grossly inadequate hydration, absence of fibre-containing nutrients, and level of physical exercise. All patients must receive similar baseline recommendations and/or training regarding nutrition, fluid intake, and exercise.
rules.verbatim_quote: no strong recommendations can be given. However, all patients should be evaluated for grossly inadequate hydration, total absence of fibre-containing nutrients, and level of physical exercise. All patients should receive similar baseline recommendations and/or training regarding nutrition, fluid intake and exercise.

rules.rule_text: OIC patients enrolled in clinical trials must have an intake of opioids of at least 3 months, an onset of constipation after the start of opioid therapy, and must satisfy the same Rome III-derived inclusion criteria as for chronic idiopathic constipation. A minimum daily opioid dose threshold and a stable dosing period prior to inclusion must be specified.
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients with opioid-induced constipation should have an intake of opioids of at least 3 months, and an onset of constipation after the start of opioid therapy. A certain threshold for the minimal daily dose of opioids should be applied and a stable dosing of opioids should be made a requirement for a certain period prior to inclusion into trials in patients with non-cancer pain.
rules.exceptions: For cancer pain patients, stable dosing can only partly be achieved; a more flexible approach is acceptable. Patients with severe aggravation of constipation symptoms after starting opioids may be included but are not recommended due to difficulty distinguishing aggravation from pre-existing disease.

rules.rule_text: For a full OIC indication where the main part of the evidence comes from trials in non-malignant pain patients, at least one additional safety trial in the malignant pain population is required. Extrapolation of efficacy from non-cancer to cancer pain patients is acceptable based on a sufficiently large high-dose opioid subgroup.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1.2 — Cancer-pain and non-cancer pain patients
rules.verbatim_quote: If the main part of the evidence of a development programme is expected from trials in non-malignant pain patients: (An) additional safety trial(s) in the malignant pain population will be required for a full indication of OIC. The transfer of efficacy from non-cancer pain patients to cancer pain patients should be based on a sufficiently large subgroup from the studies in non-cancer pain patients, who are treated with high doses of opioids. If a sufficient number of high-dose patients has not been included in these studies, additional short-term efficacy studies should be conducted.
rules.exceptions: If main evidence is expected from cancer pain trials: full extrapolation of efficacy will not be acceptable; additional study(ies) in non-cancer pain OIC patients will be necessary.

rules.rule_text: Patients entering OIC studies with malignant pain must have a diagnosis of confirmed neoplasm and a life expectancy of at least 3 months.
rules.source_section: Section 5.1.2 — Studies in cancer pain patients
rules.verbatim_quote: Patients entering studies in OIC with malignant pain should have a diagnosis of confirmed neoplasm, but with a life expectancy of at least 3 months.
rules.exceptions: For cancer pain patients, the requirement of stable dosing and certain restrictions applicable to non-cancer pain patients should not be applied; a broad range of performance status is acceptable as long as life expectancy criterion is fulfilled.

rules.rule_text: In bowel cleansing clinical trials, the results of the diagnostic evaluation and the underlying pathology of enrolled patients must be reported, in order to at least assess the safety of patients with bowel pathology in comparison to those without.
rules.verbatim_quote: the results/outcome of the diagnostic evaluation – and thus the 'underlying pathology' – in the patients included in such trials should in future be reported, in order to at least assess the safety of patients with bowel pathology in comparison to those without. For the inclusion of 'at risk-patients', see Chapter 8.

rules.rule_text: During early development of CIC/OIC drug candidates, gastrointestinal transit measurement (preferably using radiopaque markers; alternatively radionuclides/gamma scintigraphy or wireless capsule methods) must be performed in healthy volunteers and in patients, with separate measurements for STC and NTC patients.
rules.source_section: Section 5.2.1 — Chronic idiopathic constipation and opioid induced constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: The measurement of gastrointestinal transit (and/or parts of total transit; usually colonic transit) should be done in healthy volunteers and in patients (separate for patients with STC and NTC). The method of choice appears to be the use of radiopaque markers for which a variety of methods are available. Alternatively radionuclides with gamma scintigraphy or wireless capsule methods (based on pH evaluation) or a combination of these may be used.

rules.rule_text: During early-phase CIC/OIC trials, gender differential effects must be adequately evaluated. Both men and women must be included already at this stage of development.
rules.source_section: Section 5.2.1 — Chronic idiopathic constipation and opioid induced constipation
rules.verbatim_quote: During these early trials, a differential effect regarding gender should also be adequately evaluated. Therefore, both men and women should be included already at this stage of development.

rules.rule_text: During early development of new bowel cleansing purgative substances or combinations, full monitoring of cardiovascular parameters (blood pressure, heart rate), renal function (eGFR), serum chemistry (electrolytes, pH, acid-base balance), and body fluid composition (stool and urine: total amount/weight, osmolality, electrolyte content, net water balance) is required.
rules.verbatim_quote: The application of either a large amount of fluid to, or the production of such by the body itself in the bowels may theoretically have an influence on a variety of parameters, encompassing not only cardiovascular (blood pressure, heart beat frequency), and renal function (serum measurements, eGFR), as well as serum chemistry (electrolytes including magnesium, phosphorus and calcium, pH, and acid-base balance), and the composition of body fluids such as stool and urine (total amount/weight, osmolality, electrolyte content/composition, total net water balance). A full monitoring of the total of these functions will be necessary during the early development of new purgative regimens.

rules.rule_text: Systemically available constipation or bowel cleansing compounds with an identified receptor target must evaluate drug-drug interactions according to the relevant interaction guideline.
rules.verbatim_quote: If compounds – whether used for the treatment of constipation or for bowel cleansing – are systemically available and do exert their effects not or not only on the basis of osmotic effects (i.e. a receptor target is identified) the evaluation of interactions should be done similar to other compounds according to the respective guideline.

rules.rule_text: All constipation and bowel cleansing compounds, regardless of whether they have an identified receptor target, must have their drug-drug interaction potential addressed via mechanism-based interaction studies. Oral contraceptives must be specifically tested as a model widely-used compound.
rules.verbatim_quote: All substances, however, whether fulfilling the above criteria or not, will be subject to a suspicion of drug interactions due to their pharmacodynamic action, in as the influence on motility and secretion may also influence absorption of other compounds. Although the potential problem is largely unexplored and only a few data are available, this should in future be addressed in respective drug-drug interaction studies based on the mechanism of action of the compound, and potentially in in-vitro experiments which could help to determine adequate test substances. Oral contraceptives are regarded to represent a good example for a widely used compound to be tested.

rules.rule_text: A full exploration of the dose-response relationship is generally expected for all constipation and bowel cleansing compounds, following ICH E4 (CPMP/ICH/378/95).
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, for this part of the development, consideration should be given to the Guideline 'Dose response Information to Support Drug Registration' (ICH E4, CPMP/ICH/378/95). A full exploration of the dose-response relationship is generally expected.

rules.rule_text: For compounds with a specific opioid-antagonising mechanism of action, dose finding must include exploration of the full range of potential opioid doses and must determine whether a specific agonist/antagonist activity ratio at the μ-opioid receptor, or a specific dose level ('one size fits all'), confers optimal pharmacodynamic activity.
rules.verbatim_quote: For compounds with a specific opioid-antagonising mechanism of action, this needs to include the exploration of the coverage of the full range of potential opioid-doses, and the answer to the question whether a specific ratio of the agonistic/antagonistic activity at the μ-opioid receptor, or a specific dose level ('one size fits all') confers optimal pharmacodynamics activity.

rules.rule_text: Confirmatory CIC trials must be double-blind, parallel group, with a minimum duration of 3 months. Duration may need to be extended to 6 months depending on mechanism and speed of onset of action. An additional study period of at least 4 weeks must be added to evaluate withdrawal and/or rebound, best addressed with a randomised withdrawal phase.
rules.verbatim_quote: Double-blind, parallel group clinical trials should be performed. The trials should be long enough to determine if any response will be sustained, and to cover a potential late drop-out. The duration of such studies is recommended to be at least 3 months. Depending on the mechanism and speed of onset of action, the study may need a duration of up to 6 months. After the initial treatment period, an additional study period of at least 4 weeks should be added evaluating withdrawal and/or rebound, which can be best addressed with a randomised withdrawal phase.
rules.exceptions: Other study designs/durations must be justified in terms of adequately assessing long-term sustained efficacy, withdrawal, rebound, and safety.

rules.rule_text: All CIC confirmatory trials must include a run-in phase of at least 2 weeks during which any previous active treatment (except a defined rescue medication) is withdrawn and full compliance with the inclusion criteria is documented.
rules.verbatim_quote: All trials should include a run-in phase of at least 2 weeks, during which any previous active treatment is withdrawn, except a defined rescue medication, and the full compliance with the inclusion criteria is documented. In case non-response to 'usual laxatives' is needed to be documented (see Chapter 4.3.), the active therapy with insufficient response has, of course to be maintained during the run-in phase.
rules.exceptions: If previous laxative failure must be documented (per Section 4.3), the insufficiently effective active therapy must be maintained during run-in.

rules.rule_text: The comparator in CIC confirmatory trials is generally placebo, which is normally sufficient to conclude on the overall benefit-risk balance. Consideration must be given to conducting at least one trial with an active comparator; if used, the active comparator arm should document non-inferiority (with assay sensitivity confirmation) or superiority.
rules.verbatim_quote: As a general rule, the comparator which is required in such studies is placebo and the comparison to placebo will normally be sufficient to conclude on the overall benefit-risk- balance of a product. However, consideration should be given to conducting at least one trial with the use of an additional active comparator, due to the wide availability of such compounds and their proven standard of care status, widespread use, and well-established safety.
rules.exceptions: If assay sensitivity is documented without non-inferiority documentation, and specific comparative labelling claims are intended, the evaluation must focus on advantages in other domains (e.g. safety, administration schedule).

rules.rule_text: The appropriate comparator for confirmatory studies in opioid induced constipation is placebo.
rules.verbatim_quote: The appropriate comparator for studies in opioid induced constipation is considered to be placebo.

rules.rule_text: Studies for bowel cleansing before colonoscopy must be randomised, active-controlled studies (placebo-controlled designs are not ethically justifiable or possible). The aim must be to demonstrate at least non-inferiority. If a non-inferiority design is used, the non-inferiority margin must not be based on the difference of any active bowel cleansing medication versus placebo, but must be clinically justified based on a clinically acceptable difference in the endpoint used.
rules.verbatim_quote: Studies for bowel cleansing before colonoscopy are requested to be active controlled studies, because the administration of a 'placebo regimen' is not possible or can ethically not be justified. [...] It is therefore considered necessary to conduct, randomised, controlled studies, in which the aim should be to demonstrate at least non-inferiority of the new substance/compound/regimen. In case a non-inferiority study is conducted the choice of the non-inferiority margin should not be based on the difference of (any) active bowel cleansing medication to placebo but on a clinically acceptable difference of the endpoint used. This could be justified on the theoretical assumption of missed pathology with a poor bowel preparation. In any case, the proposed non-inferiority margin should be clinically justified.
rules.exceptions: Blinding must be included whenever possible; where different fluid volumes are administered, blinding may not be achievable.

rules.rule_text: The primary efficacy endpoint for CIC and OIC confirmatory trials must be a responder analysis using complete spontaneous bowel movements (CSBMs), defining a responder as achieving at least 3 CSBMs/week with an increase of at least 1 CSBM/week compared to baseline. The primary evaluation must be based on an overall 75% response rate relative to the total duration of the study (in weeks), including 'sustained response' defined as fulfilling these criteria for the last 4 weeks of treatment.
rules.source_section: Section 5.6.2 — Endpoints (Chronic idiopathic constipation and opioid induced constipation)
rules.verbatim_quote: a responder analysis is recommended which takes into consideration a response defined as at least 3 CSBMs/week and at the same time an increase of at least 1 CSBM/week compared to the baseline period. The primary evaluation should be based on an overall 75% response rate related to the total duration of the study (in weeks), including 'sustained response' defined as fulfilling these criteria for the last 4 weeks of treatment.
rules.exceptions: In OIC cancer pain patients, where recruitment may be difficult, a numerical scale based on bowel movement frequency (with additional features such as spontaneity, completeness, and normal consistency BSFS 3-4) comparing change from baseline to end of treatment is considered acceptable. Use of other primary endpoints requires a thorough justification including argumentation based on available evidence/validation data.

rules.rule_text: A comprehensive evaluation of secondary endpoints must be part of CIC and OIC trials. These must include: frequency of CSBMs and SBMs (numerical), stool consistency (Bristol Stool Form Scale), further symptoms (straining, completeness of evacuation, anorectal blockage, pain, manual manoeuvres), partially validated constipation scales (e.g. BFI for OIC, BF-diary, PAC-SYM), global scales (PGIC, satisfaction with bowel habits), QoL instruments (generic: SF-36/SF-12; disease-specific: PAC-QOL), rescue treatment use, and time to first SBM or CSBM after first administration.
rules.source_section: Section 5.6.2 — Endpoints (Chronic idiopathic constipation and opioid induced constipation)
rules.verbatim_quote: a comprehensive evaluation of secondary endpoints should be part of the trials. These may comprise the following: The evaluation of the frequency of CSBMs and SBMs (numerical evaluation); The evaluation of stool consistency (with the Bristo Stool Form Scale; BSFS); The evaluation of further symptoms such as straining, completeness of evacuations, sensations of anorectal blockage, pain and discomfort and manual manoeuvres (e.g. 'ease of passage' on a 5-7-point Likert scale; dichotomous evaluation for manual moeuvres etc.); Partially validated scales that assess constipation symptoms (such as Bowel Function Index (BFI) (OIC only), Bowel Function-Diary (BF-diary) etc), PAC-SYM, etc; Global scales such as 'Global Impression of Change' (PGIC), assessment of 'satisfaction with bowel habits' etc.; Measurements of Quality of Life on generic (e.g. SF-36; SF-12) as well as disease specific instruments (e.g. PAC-QOL); The use of rescue treatment; Time to first SBM or CSBM after the first administration.

rules.rule_text: The primary endpoint for bowel cleansing trials must be a validated cleanliness scale. Acceptable validated scales include the Boston Bowel Preparation Scale (BBPS), Aronchick scale, Ottawa scale, and Harefield Cleansing Scale. Scales without respective validation will not be considered acceptable. As a secondary evaluation, a segmental evaluation of the cleanliness of the bowel must be used.
rules.verbatim_quote: The choice of the scale to be used as a primary endpoint should in all cases be justified and discussed, and the respective validation exercises be presented. Scales without respective validation will not be considered to be acceptable. Because usually these scales attribute different ordinal scales on the different segments of the colon, a segmental evaluation of the cleanliness of the bowel should be used as secondary evaluation.

rules.rule_text: A secondary evaluation of bowel preparation acceptability to patients must be included, assessing global impression on taste, willingness to repeat administration, and palatability surveys. Completeness of intake must also be assessed as a secondary endpoint. Additional secondary endpoints may include insertion and withdrawal times and adenoma detection rate.
rules.verbatim_quote: Efficacy of bowel preparations should – as a secondary evaluation – also be assessed in terms of acceptability to the patients. This can be done by simple questions on global impression on taste, willingness for repeat administration, and surveys of palatability (e.g. in case fluids are administered). A secondary evaluation – outside of the tolerability evaluations – is also the completeness of the intake. Further secondary endpoints may include insertion and withdrawal as well as total colonoscopy time, and the adenoma (or other irregularity) detection rate.

rules.rule_text: Separate, generally double-blind, placebo-controlled clinical trials in children are required for CIC drug development due to the different underlying pathophysiology of childhood constipation. Inclusion criteria must fully reflect the Rome III/PACCT definitions. All age ranges between 0 and 18 years must be included in sufficient numbers.
rules.verbatim_quote: Due to the different underlying pathophysiology for most of the cases of childhood constipation, the conduct of separate trials in children is generally considered to be necessary. Separate dose-finding trials may also be necessary depending on the mechanism of action. [...] The inclusion of all age ranges (in sufficient numbers) between 0 and 18 years is considered to be necessary and clinical trials should usually be conducted double-blind and placebo-controlled. The inclusion criteria should fully reflect the Rome III/PACCT definitions.
rules.exceptions: For locally acting drugs, dose finding may be especially challenging; animal or in-vitro models and experience with established treatments may be used. In case of anticipated recruitment problems, partial extrapolation and interpolation may be justified based on underlying pathophysiology and conducted trials.

rules.rule_text: Paediatric CIC efficacy trials may be limited to 8 weeks in duration only in cases where longer-term efficacy data are already available in adults.
rules.verbatim_quote: The duration of efficacy trials in the paediatric population can be 8 weeks only in cases where longer-term data are already available in adults.

rules.rule_text: Open-label extension trials of 10 months duration must be conducted in the paediatric population to document long-term safety (achieving a 12-month overall treatment duration).
rules.verbatim_quote: to take full account of the patients not recovering and thus being in need of long-term therapy and to document long-term safety open-label extension trials should be conducted for a duration of 10 months (in order to make up for a 12 month overall duration of treatment).

rules.rule_text: All studies covered by this guideline must recruit older patients (those above 65 and/or above 75) at rates that are at least representative of the natural age range distribution. In Phase III trials, at least informative rates of recruitment for activity and safety in patients aged above 65 are necessary.
rules.verbatim_quote: The recruitment of older patients – those above 65 and/or those above 75 – is therefore considered desirable for all studies included in this guideline at rates that are at least representative of the natural age range distribution. In phase III trials, therefore at least rates of recruitment that are informative about activity and safety in those aged above 65 will be necessary.
rules.exceptions: Depending on theoretical safety risks and early development safety results, special safety studies in even more vulnerable populations (e.g. institutionalised/frail older people) are recommended.

rules.rule_text: CIC confirmatory trials must aim to recruit at least approximately 30% of their patient population from the male gender, in order to be representative.
rules.verbatim_quote: In the future, trials should aim at recruiting at least about 30% of their patient population from the male gender, in order to be representative.

rules.rule_text: The omission of either male or female patients from the development programme is not considered desirable, unless clearly justified by grossly reduced expectance of efficacy or grossly increased potential risks. If a relevant gender difference is not expected, the population must still be stratified according to sex.
rules.verbatim_quote: An omission of either male or female patients from the development is not considered to be desirable, unless clearly justified by grossly reduced expectance of efficacy, or grossly increased potential risks. [...] If a relevant difference is not expected, nevertheless, the population should be stratified according to sex.
rules.exceptions: Omission justified only by grossly reduced efficacy or grossly increased potential risks.

rules.rule_text: For global development programmes in CIC and bowel cleansing, a sufficient proportion of patients must be recruited in Europe unless it can be demonstrated that this is not necessary. Justification must be based on analysis of ethnic/geographic and cultural factors per ICH E5 (EMA/CHMP/EWP/692792/2008) and presented at the time of MAA.
rules.verbatim_quote: For 'global developments', recruiting patients from several regions of the world, the inclusion of a sufficient proportion of patients recruited in Europe is recommended unless it can be demonstrated that this is not necessary. This justification should be based on the analysis of ethnic/geographic and cultural factors according to the requirements of the respective guidance documents (ICH E 5, EMA/CHMP/EWP/692792/2008) should be presented at the time of MAA.
rules.exceptions: For CIC and bowel cleansing, where mainly European descent populations are included, transfer of data from North American to European populations appears possible.

rules.rule_text: For the OIC indication, a relevant proportion of patients must be recruited in Europe or from regions with comparable opioid prescribing practice, because US opioid prescribing patterns (especially for non-malignant diseases) differ substantially from European patterns, resulting in potentially different safety profiles.
rules.verbatim_quote: This is considered to be different for the condition of opioid-induced constipation for patients recruited in the US. Prescribing of opioids – especially for non-malignant diseases – is considered to vary widely between the US and Europe. [...] the inclusion of a relevant proportion of patients recruited in Europe – or from regions with comparable prescribing practice of opioids – is recommended in the indication OIC.

rules.rule_text: Long-term safety data with an observation period of at least 12 months must be available in adequate patient numbers for CIC and OIC medicinal products, because these conditions require intermittent or continuous long-term use of medication. The Note for Guidance on Population Exposure (CHMP/ICH/375/95) is fully applicable.
rules.source_section: Section 7 — Safety (Chronic Idiopathic Constipation and Opioid Induced Constipation)
rules.verbatim_quote: The treatment of CIC and OIC will require intermittent or continuous long-term use of medication, and it is therefore necessary to have long-term safety data with an observation period of at least 12 months available in adequate numbers to accurately assess the safety of a medicinal product. The Note for Guidance on Population Exposure: The Extent of Population Exposure to assess Clinical Safety (CHMP/ICH/375/95) is considered to be fully applicable.
rules.exceptions: Bowel cleansing purgatives: no long-term safety studies are necessary (see EMA-CCBC-046).

rules.rule_text: Safety evaluations for CIC and OIC products must focus on: induction of diarrhoea and its consequences (net water loss/dehydration, electrolyte changes, acid/base balance); potential cardiovascular consequences of water and electrolyte changes (changes in heart rate and blood pressure); and hypotension and syncope as special events.
rules.source_section: Section 7 — Safety (Chronic Idiopathic Constipation and Opioid Induced Constipation)
rules.verbatim_quote: The evaluation of safety should therefore focus on the induction of diarrhoea and the consequences hereof, namely the loss/change in net water (dehydration), electrolytes, acid/base balance. Also potential consequences of water and electrolyte changes/loss like change in heart beat and blood pressure, as well as hypotension and syncope as special events, should be in the focus of the safety investigations.

rules.rule_text: The potential for laxative abuse must be part of the Risk Management Plan, with appropriate observational studies proposed to be conducted post-licensing. Pre-approval safety studies alone are insufficient to adequately address laxative abuse potential.
rules.source_section: Section 7 — Safety (Chronic Idiopathic Constipation and Opioid Induced Constipation)
rules.verbatim_quote: The potential of laxative abuse – one of the oldest problems in healthcare – should be assessed based on the PD properties and the results of the safety evaluations during development. However, laxative abuse can hardly be addressed with safety studies before licensing. Therefore, laxative abuse should be part of the Risk Management Plan with appropriate observational studies to be proposed to be conducted post-licensing.

rules.rule_text: For OIC products where the pharmacological mechanism of action targets opioid receptors in the gastrointestinal tract, special emphasis must be placed on evaluating: (a) the induction of opioid withdrawal symptoms, and (b) the impact on pain symptoms reflecting a potential interaction of the opioid antagonist on the central analgesic effect. Subgroups according to types of opioid treatment must be analysed.
rules.verbatim_quote: Special emphasis in the treatment of opioid-induced constipation – especially if the pharmacological mechanism of action is targeted at the opioid receptors in the gastrointestinal tract – has to be paid on the induction of opioid withdrawal symptoms, and on the impact on pain symptoms reflecting a potential interaction of opioid antagonist on the central analgesic effect. Subgroups according to the types of opioid treatment should be analysed.

rules.rule_text: For bowel cleansing purgatives (usually administered once), no long-term safety studies are necessary. However, evaluation of safety parameters (water/electrolyte balance, acid-base balance, cardiovascular effects) is of utmost importance. Safety in relevant theoretical risk populations (hepatic and renal impairment, heart disease, pre-defined bowel disease such as IBD) must be evaluated once safe use in an otherwise healthy population has been established.
rules.verbatim_quote: For purgatives - where the administration is usually only once for considerable periods of time - no long-term safety studies are necessary. However, the evaluation of safety parameters as mentioned above is considered to be of utmost importance. Special emphasis should be paid to the inclusion of relevant theoretical risk populations, once the safe use in an 'otherwise' healthy population has been established. Depending on the mode of action, this may include patients with hepatic and renal impairment, heart disease, and pre-defined bowel disease (e.g. IBD).
